query: html form default method interview question

competitors.label: <form>: The Form element
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN reference for the form element, including the method attribute, GET defaults, and how browsers submit form data.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on form submission semantics, GET and POST behavior, and the baseline structure of the form element.
theirGaps: The reference format is less focused on combining the method fallback, omitted-action behavior, and GET-versus-POST security nuance into a compact interview answer.

realWorldUseCases: An HTML form defaults to GET when method is missing or invalid. If action is also omitted, it submits to the current page URL and serializes successful controls into the query string. Use GET for read-only navigation and POST for state-changing or sensitive submissions, together with HTTPS and server-side validation.
followUpConfusion: POST places form data in the request body, which keeps it out of the address bar but does not encrypt it or make it private by itself.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains form submission semantics well, while our page surfaces the practical risk: GET places successful controls in a URL that may be retained in browser history, logs, and analytics.
actionableExamples: It includes canonical form examples and method notes, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct about two common follow-ups: an omitted action targets the current page URL, and POST does not provide encryption by itself.

actionableExamples: Keep the GET-versus-POST comparison visible, including request location and HTTPS nuance, so the answer stays practical rather than only definitional.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical form reference, but our page is stronger as a compact interview answer because it combines the GET fallback, omitted-action target, query-string result, and POST security nuance.

realWorldUseCases: An HTML form defaults to GET when method is missing or invalid. Use GET for read-only navigation and POST for state-changing or sensitive submissions, together with HTTPS and server-side validation.
followUpConfusion: POST places form data in the request body, which keeps it out of the address bar but does not encrypt it or make it private by itself.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is relevant for HTML prep, but the category page is less explicit about the current-page target created by an omitted action and the URL exposure created by default GET.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong interview bank, so the worked-example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page answers the likely follow-ups directly: what an omitted action targets and why moving data to a POST request body is not the same as securing it.

actionableExamples: Keep the no-method, no-action form and resulting current-page URL visible so the answer stays anchored to observable browser behavior.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is broad HTML prep, but our page is more specific about the method and action defaults, the resulting query string, and the controls still required when POST is used.

competitors.label: Use forms to get data from users

theirStrengths: It is strong on practical form submission behavior and on showing how GET and POST differ in real requests.
theirGaps: It is broader than the exact trivia question because it covers the whole form element workflow, not only the default method.

realWorldUseCases: An HTML form defaults to GET when method is missing or invalid. If action is also omitted, it submits to the current page URL and serializes successful controls into the query string. Use GET for read-only navigation and POST for state-changing or sensitive submissions, together with HTTPS and server-side validation.
followUpConfusion: POST places form data in the request body, which keeps it out of the address bar but does not encrypt it or make it private by itself.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev is grounded in real submission behavior, while our answer keeps the omitted-method and omitted-action fallbacks together with the resulting query-string URL.
actionableExamples: The tutorial includes concrete GET and POST examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
